L0_description: The narrative provides clear evidence of human error. The jumper chose to jump despite obviously dangerous wind conditions (sustained strong gusts, flags horizontal, conditions described as 'terrible') that two companions recognized as unsafe and declined. The jumper was explicitly warned by a companion that it was not worth it. The decision to jump in these conditions constitutes poor judgment and risk acceptance. The wind conditions were clearly observable and forecastable before the jump, ruling out an unforeseeable environmental event.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Poor judgment or risk acceptance
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Brett chose to jump in clearly dangerous wind conditions — sustained strong gusts with flags horizontal — after waiting 45 minutes with no improvement. Two companions declined to jump and explicitly warned him it was not worth it, yet he launched anyway. This decision to jump in winds that could overpower his canopy directly produced the fatal object strike.

L2_preconditions.label: Pressure/Haste/Misplaced Motivation
L2_preconditions.description: Brett was on his last day in Lauterbrunnen and described as 'desperate' to get one final jump before his flight home the next morning. This time pressure and misplaced motivation to complete the jump drove him to proceed despite clearly dangerous conditions and explicit warnings from companions.

L0_description: The fatal outcome resulted from the bridle being misrouted around the right leg strap, a rigging error that prevented canopy extraction and caused a total malfunction (PC in tow). This is a human error in packing/rigging, compounded by the failure of both the jumper and his companion to perform a pre-jump gear check that would have immediately caught the misrouting.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Bridle misrouted around leg strap
L1_unsafe_acts.description: During packing/rigging the day before the jump, the jumper misrouted the bridle around the right leg strap. The PC inflated normally after deployment but the bridle was physically locked against the leg strap, preventing canopy extraction from the container. This rigging execution error directly caused a total malfunction and closed-container impact.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to perform pre-jump gear check
L2_preconditions.description: Neither the jumper nor his companion performed a pre-jump gear check at the exit point. The companion explicitly states that running a hand along the bridle routing would have immediately caught the misrouted bridle, which would have prevented the fatal jump.

L2_preconditions.label: Inattention during packing
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper packed in a rush the evening before because he wanted to go to sleep early to catch early morning conditions. This rushed, inattentive state during packing directly contributed to the bridle misrouting error, as the task of routing the bridle correctly requires careful attention to the deployment chain.

L0_description: The jumper chose to fly a wingsuit proximity line in conditions of complete flat light (heavy fresh snow, no shadows, no contrast, no texture) that eliminated all depth cues, which constitutes a decision error. He then failed to maintain altitude awareness during flight, flying into terrain at full speed with no deployment attempt, indicating a failure to monitor critical information (altitude/terrain proximity). Human error in both the decision to jump in those visual conditions and the failure to deploy is clearly present.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Attention failure - altitude awareness
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper failed to monitor and correctly assess his altitude during wingsuit flight, making no deployment initiation or any change in body position prior to impact. Helmet cam footage confirms a relaxed posture with no urgency, indicating he did not attend to altitude cues. Flat light conditions eliminated depth cues, but the jumper failed to recognize the loss of visual altitude references and did not compensate by deploying at a conservative altitude.

L2_preconditions.label: Spatial Disorientation
L2_preconditions.description: Complete flat light conditions — heavy fresh snow with no shadows, no contrast, and no texture on the snowfield — eliminated all visual depth cues, causing the jumper to misperceive his altitude and distance from terrain. The narrative states he clearly believed he had plenty of altitude and perceived himself to be much higher than he was, consistent with a visual illusion induced by featureless, low-contrast terrain.

L2_preconditions.label: Degraded visual conditions
L2_preconditions.description: Heavy fresh snow, low-level clouds, and complete flat light created a featureless white-on-white environment that physically removed the visual altitude references the jumper needed to monitor terrain proximity. This environmental condition directly degraded his ability to perceive altitude, enabling the attention failure in which he flew into terrain without any deployment attempt.

L2_preconditions.label: Overconfidence
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper chose to fly a new wingsuit line on an expedition in conditions of heavy fresh snow and complete flat light — conditions known to eliminate depth cues over snowfields. Despite 90 wingsuit BASE jumps and 5 BASE seasons providing sufficient knowledge to recognize flat light as a lethal hazard, he proceeded to fly proximity terrain without adopting compensatory measures such as conservative altitude margins, suggesting an unreasonable overestimation of his ability to maintain altitude awareness in these conditions.

L0_description: The fatal outcome resulted from a latent manufacturing defect in the harness webbing (incorrect heat treatment during production) that caused internal nylon degradation undetectable by any non-destructive inspection. The jumper's packing, rigging, maintenance, equipment selection, and execution were all correct and within accepted practice. This constitutes an equipment failure independent of human error—a material defect beyond the jumper's reasonable ability to anticipate or control.

The narrative provides positive evidence that the fatal outcome resulted from an unforeseeable environmental event: a sudden localized rotor caused by a temperature inversion and differential heating that could not have been predicted or detected from the exit point. Meteorologists confirmed the event was not forecastable. The jumper's exit, tracking, deployment, and packing were all within accepted practice, and no human error is identified.

The fatal outcome resulted from a massive brainstem stroke caused by a congenital arterial malformation that had never produced symptoms and was undetected on a recent physical. The jumper had no prior medical history, no known risk factors, and no reasonable basis to anticipate the event. This constitutes a sudden medical event outside the jumper's reasonable control or anticipation, with no human error contributing to the outcome.

The narrative provides clear evidence of multiple human errors contributing to the fatal outcome. The jumper chose to jump in conditions that four experienced companions deemed unsafe (winds 20 knots sustained with gusts over 30 knots from the side), constituting a decision error. Additionally, the jumper executed an unstable exit into the crosswind gust and failed to prioritize deploying the pilot chute over recovering stability during the subsequent tumble, constituting performance/skill-based errors.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Poor judgment or risk acceptance
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper chose to jump in 20-knot sustained winds with gusts over 30 knots from the side, conditions that four experienced companions unanimously deemed unsafe and stood down from. This decision to jump in clearly excessive crosswind conditions directly produced the fatal outcome by exposing the jumper to wind forces that pushed him back toward the wall.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Technique error on exit
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper executed an unstable exit, going from stable to head-low within the first second and tumbling along the face. The poor exit technique resulted in insufficient forward separation from the wall and an unrecoverable body position, directly contributing to the fatal impact with a ledge 80 meters below.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failure to deploy pilot chute
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Despite tumbling for three to four seconds along the cliff face, the jumper never got a hand to his pilot chute. He failed to prioritize deployment over stability recovery, resulting in a subterminal closed-container impact with the ledge.

L2_preconditions.label: Strong crosswind gust degraded exit stability
L2_preconditions.description: A strong crosswind gust caught the jumper immediately upon exit, pushing him sideways and back toward the wall and contributing to his transition from stable to head-low within the first second. The wind force actively degraded his ability to execute a stable exit and maintain forward separation from the cliff face.

L2_preconditions.label: Channelized attention on stability recovery
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative states the jumper never got a hand to his PC during three to four seconds of tumbling, indicating he failed to prioritize deployment over recovering stability. This fixation on regaining body position to the exclusion of the critical task of deploying the pilot chute directly enabled the failure-to-deploy error.

L0_description: Multiple human errors contributed to the fatal outcome. The jumper chose to jump while severely impaired from heavy alcohol consumption the night before (vomiting, unsteady, splitting headache), executed an unstable exit due to impaired coordination (legs buckled on push-off), and then fixated on stability recovery rather than deploying the pilot chute during the available freefall time, resulting in closed-container impact with terrain.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable exit due to poor technique
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Chris's legs buckled on the push-off, causing him to go immediately unstable and roll onto his back into a slow tumble. This technique failure at exit initiated the fatal sequence by placing him in an unrecoverable body position throughout freefall.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failure to deploy pilot chute
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Chris spent the entire approximately 6 seconds of freefall attempting to recover stability rather than deploying his pilot chute. He fixated on body position recovery and never extracted the PC from the BOC, resulting in closed-container impact with the talus. Even from an unstable position, deploying the PC could have produced a canopy.

L2_preconditions.label: Substance Effects
L2_preconditions.description: Chris was out until 4am drinking heavily the night before, was clearly still impaired at the time of the jump with a splitting headache, vomiting twice on the trail, and visible unsteadiness. The narrative explicitly states the hangover affected his coordination, which directly degraded his physical ability to execute a stable exit.

L2_preconditions.label: Channelized Attention/Fixation
L2_preconditions.description: Chris fixated entirely on recovering stability during the approximately 6 seconds of freefall to the exclusion of monitoring altitude and deploying his pilot chute. The narrative states he spent the entire freefall trying to get back to belly rather than pitching his PC, demonstrating classic channelized attention on one task while neglecting the more critical task of deployment.

L2_preconditions.label: Allowing for progressively riskier behavior
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative states Chris had jumped hung over before without issues and that the decision to jump in this state was not unusual for him. This indicates a sustained pattern of jumping while impaired by alcohol that he was aware of and failed to correct, which enabled the degraded physical performance on this exit.

L0_description: Multiple human errors contributed to the fatal outcome. The jumper attempted a tracking suit BASE jump from an intermediate-to-advanced cliff with only two skydives in the suit and no prior cliff jumps with it, representing grossly insufficient preparation. He chose an exit requiring effective tracking despite lacking the skill to fly the suit, resulting in inadequate forward separation and subterminal impact. Additional factors include driving through the night causing visible exhaustion and disregarding a more experienced jumper's suggestion to start with a more forgiving exit.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Inadequate tracking suit technique
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper exited with a decent push but was unable to achieve forward drive in the tracking suit, falling at a steep angle and failing to gain adequate separation from the cliff. The narrative states 'the tracking suit was flying him, not the other way around' and he 'could not get forward drive,' indicating he attempted the correct action (tracking away from the wall) but failed in execution due to lack of proficiency with the suit.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient tracking suit training
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had only two skydives in the tracking suit and had never jumped it from a cliff. Accepted practice requires 40-50 tracking suit skydives before BASE use, and mastery of the suit's body position for maximum forward speed and glide. This massive training deficit directly produced his inability to achieve forward drive in the suit.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient BASE experience for slider-up cliffs
L2_preconditions.description: With 40 BASE jumps all from lower exits, the jumper had no experience with slider-up cliff jumping requiring sub-terminal tracking. The exit required mandatory tracking past a technical section where the wall comes back in, a task demanding sub-terminal tracking proficiency that his jump history had never developed.

L2_preconditions.label: Physical exhaustion from overnight drive
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper drove 14 hours straight through the night and was visibly exhausted at the exit. Physical exhaustion degrades coordination and motor performance, directly impairing his ability to execute the demanding body position control required to fly the tracking suit effectively.

L2_preconditions.label: Overconfidence from video observation
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper rejected a suggestion to start with a more forgiving exit, stating the chosen exit 'looked easy from the footage.' With zero cliff tracking suit experience and only two skydives in the suit, his assessment that the exit was easy reflects an unreasonable overestimation of his own capability relative to the specific task, beyond what a reasonable jumper in his position would conclude.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to correct tracking suit proficiency gap
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper purchased the tracking suit a month before but completed only two skydives in it, far below the 40-50 tracking suit skydives accepted as minimum before BASE use. He was aware he owned new equipment requiring proficiency development and failed to undertake the necessary training over the available month before attempting a cliff jump requiring mandatory tracking.

L0_description: The jumper experienced a 180-degree off-heading opening facing the building and attempted a heading correction by grabbing only the right toggle and pulling it hard, which induced a diving turn into the building rather than arresting forward speed. This constitutes a technique/skill-based error in off-heading correction procedure; accepted practice for a 180-degree off-heading near an object is to first apply both rear risers to halt forward speed before initiating a turn, or to grab both toggles to near-stall before releasing one side. The single-toggle hard input was an incorrect corrective action that directly produced the fatal object strike.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Incorrect off-heading correction technique
L1_unsafe_acts.description: After a 180° off-heading opening facing the building, the jumper grabbed only the right toggle and pulled it hard, initiating a diving turn into the building. Accepted practice for a 180° off-heading correction is to use both rear risers to halt forward speed first, then release one to turn, or to grab both toggles to near-stall before releasing one side. Pulling a single toggle hard from deep brake setting drives the canopy into a diving turn toward the object rather than arresting forward speed. The technique execution was fatally flawed.

L2_preconditions.label: Startle Response/Freeze
L2_preconditions.description: The sudden 180° off-heading opening directly facing the glass facade likely triggered a startle response, causing the jumper to grab the first toggle she could find rather than executing the trained two-riser or two-toggle arrest technique. The urgency of immediate object proximity degraded her ability to execute the correct sequence.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient object avoidance drill practice
L2_preconditions.description: With 90 BASE jumps over 3 seasons, the jumper's reaction to the 180° off-heading—grabbing a single toggle and pulling hard rather than using rear risers or both toggles to arrest forward speed—suggests insufficient practiced muscle memory for object avoidance corrections. Accepted practice emphasizes extensive repetition of heading correction drills at simple spans until the response is automatic, precisely so that under stress the correct technique is executed without conscious deliberation.

L0_description: The jumper made a conscious decision to exit in near-zero visibility conditions when all other jumpers declined, believing he could navigate by feel alone. This was a clear decision error — jumping in conditions where he could not see the terrain, leading to impact with an unseen ledge during freefall. The fog was observable and known before the jump; this was not an unforeseeable environmental event but a deliberate choice to jump in clearly unsafe conditions.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Poor judgment or risk acceptance
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper chose to exit in near-zero visibility conditions where the wall, fjord, and landing area were not visible, relying solely on his sense of heading rather than visual terrain awareness. This decision to jump when all other jumpers declined due to fog directly produced the fatal outcome, as he could not see or avoid the protruding ledge he struck during freefall.

L2_preconditions.label: Overconfidence
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper stated he had done the exit dozens of times and 'knew the heading by feel,' expressing confidence that he could navigate by feel alone without visual references. This unreasonable overestimation of his ability to safely track through zero-visibility conditions—when all other jumpers declined to jump—directly drove his decision to exit.

L0_description: The jumper interrupted his tracking flight to adjust a helmet-mounted camera, which caused him to lose his tracking body position and separation from the wall. He failed to recognize his proximity to terrain and made no corrective input or deployment attempt, resulting in impact in terminal freefall. Multiple human errors—distraction from filming equipment, loss of body position, failure to maintain situational awareness, and failure to deploy—contributed to the fatal outcome.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Decision to adjust camera mid-flight
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper decided to reach up and adjust his helmet-mounted GoPro during an active tracking flight near terrain. This voluntary action broke his tracking position and diverted his attention from the primary task of maintaining separation from the wall.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failure to maintain tracking and separation
L1_unsafe_acts.description: After reaching for the camera, the jumper's body position went flat and he drifted toward the wall without making any corrective input. Despite the ledge being in clear view for several seconds, he failed to resume tracking, correct his trajectory, or deploy his parachute, resulting in terminal impact with terrain.

L2_preconditions.label: Channelized Attention/Fixation
L2_preconditions.description: After reaching for the GoPro, the jumper appears to have become fixated on the camera adjustment to the exclusion of monitoring terrain proximity, body position, and separation from the wall. The ledge was in clear view for several seconds yet he made no corrective input, indicating his attention was channelized on the camera task rather than the critical flight parameters.

L2_preconditions.label: Distraction
L2_preconditions.description: The helmet-mounted GoPro presented an external stimulus that prompted the jumper to divert attention from the primary task of tracking away from terrain. The camera equipment created the opportunity for the mid-flight decision to adjust it, consistent with the domain reference noting that cameras are both physical snag hazards and psychological distractions.

L0_description: The jumper entered a wingsuit stall during flight due to poor angle of attack control, a known recurring skill deficiency he had acknowledged but failed to correct through additional training. This technique error in flight, combined with the conscious decision to continue proximity flying despite awareness of the problem, constitutes clear human error contributing to the fatal outcome.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Wingsuit stall from poor AoA control
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Approximately 15 seconds into the flight, the jumper allowed his angle of attack to become too high, entering a stall that converted his glide path from shallow to nearly vertical. This is the same technique error pattern he had experienced on at least four prior occasions. The stall resulted in unrecoverable altitude loss and terminal impact with terrain.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Decision to fly proximity without correcting known deficiency
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper was fully aware of a recurring and serious AoA control problem that had produced at least four near-miss stalls. He explicitly acknowledged the need to return to skydiving for remedial training but chose to continue jumping cliffs every weekend without addressing the deficiency. This conscious decision to continue proximity flying with a known critical skill gap placed him in a situation where the recurring technique error became fatal.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to correct recurring AoA control deficiency
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper experienced at least four near-miss stalls from the same AoA control problem over the preceding months. He explicitly acknowledged the deficiency and identified the corrective action (50 skydives working on stall recovery) but never followed through, continuing to jump cliffs every weekend. This sustained failure to correct a known recurring skill issue directly enabled the fatal technique error.

L2_preconditions.label: Pressure/Haste/Misplaced Motivation
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper knew he needed to pause cliff flying and return to skydiving for remedial training but kept jumping cliffs every weekend instead. The narrative describes a pattern of repeatedly putting off the acknowledged corrective action in favor of continued proximity flying, indicating a misplaced motivation to keep jumping that overrode his own rational assessment of the risk.

L0_description: The narrative provides clear evidence of human error contributing to the fatal outcome. The jumper failed to make any heading correction (no toggle or riser input) after a 90-degree off-heading opening pointed him toward guy wires, despite approximately two seconds of available reaction time. Witnesses explicitly described him freezing with arms at his sides. Additionally, the jumper's decision to jump a 900-foot antenna at night with only 60 BASE jumps and minimal antenna experience (two prior antenna jumps from taller, more forgiving structures) reflects poor judgment regarding object selection relative to experience level.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failure to perform heading correction
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Despite a 90-degree off-heading opening pointing him directly at guy wires approximately 40 feet away, the jumper made no riser or toggle input to correct heading. Multiple witnesses confirmed his arms stayed at his sides and he took no corrective action during the approximately two seconds before impacting the guy wires. The jumper failed to execute the heading correction technique that the situation demanded.

L2_preconditions.label: Startle Response/Freeze
L2_preconditions.description: Multiple witnesses reported the jumper 'completely froze the moment he saw the wires directly in front of him,' with arms remaining at his sides and no corrective action attempted during the approximately two seconds before impact. This automatic physiological freeze response directly prevented execution of the heading correction that the situation demanded.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had only 60 BASE jumps and just two prior antenna jumps, both from taller structures with guy wires further from the exit point. This limited antenna experience, particularly the lack of exposure to the tighter margins of shorter antennas with closer guy wires, likely contributed to his inability to execute an immediate heading correction under pressure. Practiced off-heading corrections from antennas with close guy wires develop the reflexive response needed to act within the narrow time window available.

L2_preconditions.label: Degraded visual conditions at night
L2_preconditions.description: The jump was conducted at night (23:30), which degraded the jumper's ability to visually acquire the guy wires and orient himself immediately after opening. Reduced visual conditions at night impair depth perception and spatial awareness, contributing to delayed recognition and response to the off-heading threat, compounding the freeze response.

L0_description: The jumper knowingly used a pilot chute with a large tear after being advised by multiple people to replace it or use a spare. This was a conscious decision error — choosing to jump with equipment he knew was damaged. The torn PC failed to inflate, resulting in a total malfunction (PC in tow / collapsed PC) and closed-container impact.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Decision to jump with damaged PC
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper knowingly chose to jump with a pilot chute that had a large tear in the mesh, despite being told it needed replacement and being offered spare PCs by other jumpers. He assessed the damage and consciously decided the torn PC would 'still catch enough air.' This decision directly produced the fatal total malfunction, as the damaged PC could not hold air, collapsed in his burble, and resulted in a closed-container impact.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to correct known equipment deficiency
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper was explicitly aware of the damaged pilot chute — he examined the tear, was told by companions it needed replacement, and was offered spare PCs. Despite this known equipment deficiency, he failed to correct it by replacing the PC before the next jump, directly enabling his decision to jump with compromised gear.

L0_description: The narrative explicitly describes multiple human errors contributing to the fatal outcome. The group failed to establish proper exit order separation, specific delays, flight paths, or opening altitudes for the multi-way jump. The deceased tracked over the second jumper and deployed directly into that jumper's inflating canopy without checking his airspace, resulting in canopy entanglement and fatal impact. These are clear decision and performance errors by both the group and the individual jumper.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Attention failure: no airspace check
L1_unsafe_acts.description: David failed to check his airspace before deploying his pilot chute, directly pitching into the second jumper's inflating canopy. The narrative explicitly states 'David did not check his airspace before deploying.' This is an attention failure — the information (another jumper's position and canopy) was available but David did not attend to it before initiating deployment.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Poor tracking separation from group
L1_unsafe_acts.description: David tracked directly over the second jumper rather than maintaining horizontal separation. The narrative states he 'tracked right over the second jumper,' indicating a technique error in tracking that placed him on a collision course at deployment altitude.

L2_preconditions.label: Inadequate briefing or coordination
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative explicitly states 'Nobody really went through the exit order or separation plan properly. We just said who would go first second third. No specific delays were set and no one talked about flight paths or opening altitudes.' This absence of coordinated separation planning directly enabled both L1 errors: without agreed flight paths, David had no defined tracking direction to maintain separation, and without coordinated opening altitudes or delays, the second jumper's canopy was inflating in David's flight path at deployment.

L0_description: The jumper deliberately delayed deployment far beyond safe limits from a span, resulting in impact with the canopy still extracting. The narrative explicitly states he 'chose to pull low' and had a pattern of progressively extending delays despite warnings from other jumpers. This constitutes a clear human error in decision-making regarding deployment altitude.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Poor judgment or risk acceptance
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper deliberately chose to deploy at a dangerously low altitude, continuing a pattern of progressively longer delays. He pulled just above the water with insufficient altitude for the canopy to complete extraction and inflation, resulting in impact with the canopy still deploying.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to correct progressively riskier behavior
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had been progressively extending his delays from 4 seconds to 6 to 8 and sometimes longer over the past season. Other jumpers had spoken to him about it multiple times, yet he failed to correct this escalating pattern of increasingly dangerous low pulls.

L0_description: The jumper flew an expert-level wingsuit (Aura 1) with only approximately 10 skydives in it, far below the accepted minimum of 40-60 suit-specific skydives before BASE use. During the flight, his angle of attack shifted and he stalled the suit, losing glide and sinking into terrain—a technique error directly linked to insufficient familiarity with the suit's stall characteristics. Multiple human errors in equipment progression decisions and in-flight technique contributed to the fatal outcome.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Poor judgment flying expert suit
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper chose to fly an expert-level wingsuit (Aura 1) off a cliff with only approximately 10 skydives in the suit, far below the accepted minimum of 40-60 skydives in a specific suit before wingsuit BASE. The narrative explicitly states the suit has stall boundaries requiring many flights to understand and he had not put in that time.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Loss of speed and stall
L1_unsafe_acts.description: During the flight, the jumper's angle of attack shifted to too high, stalling the suit and causing his glide to go from shallow to nearly vertical. He was unable to recover from the stall, sinking rapidly until terrain impact. This reflects inadequate technique in managing AoA and speed in the expert-level wingsuit.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient skydives in expert wingsuit
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had only approximately 10 skydives in the Aura 1 expert wingsuit before taking it off a cliff, far below the accepted minimum of 40-60 skydives in a specific suit before wingsuit BASE. This insufficient familiarity with the suit's flight envelope meant he lacked the knowledge base to accurately assess whether he was ready to fly it in BASE, enabling the poor judgment decision.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient experience with expert suit flight characteristics
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative explicitly states the expert-level wingsuit has stall boundaries requiring many flights to understand and the jumper had not put in that time. With only ~10 skydives in the Aura 1 and prior experience limited to intermediate suits, he lacked the trained muscle memory and kinesthetic awareness needed to manage AoA and recognize the onset of stall in this suit, directly enabling the technique error mid-flight.

L2_preconditions.label: Thermal activity degrading flight conditions
L2_preconditions.description: The weather was described as sunny and hot with thermals producing. Active thermal conditions create variable lift and sink along a flight path, which can disrupt a wingsuit pilot's speed and AoA management. For a pilot unfamiliar with the suit's stall boundaries, thermal-induced turbulence or sink could have contributed to the AoA shift and loss of glide observed mid-flight.

L0_description: The jumper attempted a BASE jump with only 5 skydives and zero BASE jumps, far below the accepted minimum of 200+ skydives and proper BASE training. This represents a catastrophic failure in progression norms. The narrative indicates a possible unstable exit leading to insufficient separation from the cliff, combined with either subterminal impact or an off-heading opening near the wall, all consistent with a complete lack of the skills and experience required for a slider-up cliff jump with a tracking suit.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Poor judgment or risk acceptance
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper attempted a slider-up tracking suit BASE jump from a cliff with only 5 skydives and zero BASE jumps, representing an extreme departure from accepted progression norms (minimum 200+ skydives before BASE, 40-50 tracking suit skydives before BASE use, 100+ BASE jumps before advanced cliff jumping). This decision to attempt the jump at all with grossly insufficient experience directly produced the fatal outcome.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Ambiguous freefall failure mechanism
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The narrative provides three possible scenarios: subterminal impact from an unstable exit, terminal impact from excessive delay, or off-heading opening into the wall after a long delay. The jumper was found with massive head injuries and canopy out, but the narrative is insufficient to determine the specific execution failure — whether it was an unstable exit, failure to deploy at the correct altitude, poor tracking/separation, or off-heading response failure.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient Skydiving Experience
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had only 5 skydives total, far below the accepted minimum of 200+ skydives before beginning BASE. This extreme lack of foundational skydiving training meant the jumper lacked the body control, canopy skills, and deployment experience necessary to evaluate the risks of the jump, directly enabling the decision to attempt it.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had zero BASE jumps and attempted a slider-up tracking suit cliff jump — an advanced discipline requiring extensive BASE progression. With no BASE experience whatsoever, the jumper had no frame of reference for assessing the demands of the jump, enabling the grossly inappropriate decision.

L2_preconditions.label: Lack of BASE specific training
L2_preconditions.description: With only 5 skydives and zero BASE jumps, the jumper could not have completed the accepted progression path (200+ skydives, FJC, mentored progression from simple objects). The absence of any structured BASE training or mentorship enabled the decision to attempt an advanced cliff jump without understanding the risks involved.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient Skydiving Experience
L2_preconditions.description: With only 5 skydives, the jumper lacked fundamental freefall body control, tracking ability, deployment technique, and canopy piloting skills. Whatever the specific execution failure was — unstable exit, poor tracking, delayed deployment, or off-heading response — the near-total absence of skydiving experience meant the jumper had none of the foundational skills required to execute any phase of the jump.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: Zero BASE jumps meant the jumper had never practiced any BASE-specific execution skill: exit technique from a cliff, sub-terminal tracking for object separation, altitude awareness in BASE context, or off-heading correction. This complete lack of BASE experience directly enabled whatever execution failure occurred during the jump.

L0_description: The jumper committed a critical packing error by placing the pilot chute inside the main container beneath the closing flaps instead of routing it to the BOC pouch. This resulted in a total malfunction (PC in tow / PC trapped in container) when the BOC handle extracted cleanly but had no pilot chute attached. Additionally, the decision to jump in deteriorating weather with increasing wind and rain, while going back and forth on whether to proceed, reflects poor judgment, though the packing error was the direct cause of the fatal outcome.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Packing error - PC misrouted
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper packed the pilot chute inside the main container underneath the closing flaps instead of routing it out to the BOC pouch. When the BOC handle was pulled during deployment, the PC remained trapped inside the closed container, resulting in a total malfunction and fatal impact.

L2_preconditions.label: Inattention during packing
L2_preconditions.description: Placing the pilot chute inside the main container instead of routing it to the BOC pouch is a gross deviation from the normal packing sequence that indicates a significant lapse in attention during packing. This fundamental misstep—packing the PC on top of the canopy under the closing flaps—suggests the jumper's attention was not adequately focused on the packing task.

L2_preconditions.label: Pressure/haste from deteriorating weather
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative describes deteriorating weather with increasing wind and rain throughout the afternoon, and the jumper was the last one at the exit going back and forth on whether to jump or hike down. This suggests the jumper was packing and preparing under time pressure from worsening conditions, which likely contributed to the rushed or distracted state that produced the packing error.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to perform pre-jump gear check
L2_preconditions.description: A standard pre-jump gear check verifying that the PC is properly routed to the BOC pouch would have caught this error before exit. The fact that the jumper exited with the PC trapped inside the container indicates that no adequate gear check was performed, or that it failed to include verification of PC routing.

L0_description: The narrative is entirely absent and no details are reported for any field including experience, equipment, weather, possible factors, or cause of death. There is no information from which to deduce either a human error pathway or a non-human cause.

The narrative provides only that the jumper impacted talus below the exit with the canopy out and no witnesses. There is insufficient information to determine whether human error contributed to the fatal outcome or whether a non-human cause was responsible. The absence of witness accounts, weather data, experience details, equipment specifics, and any description of the deployment sequence or canopy state precludes a reasonable classification.

Two experienced witnesses provide contradictory accounts of the deployment sequence — one describes a late pull while the other describes a pilot chute in tow or bridle issue after an early pull. The gear was not available for inspection, making it impossible to determine whether the fatal outcome resulted from a decision error (late pull), an equipment malfunction, or a skill-based error. Neither a human error pathway nor a non-human cause can be established with sufficient confidence.

The jumper maintained an active body position throughout the flight but made no deployment attempt, impacting in terminal freefall. The rig was recovered in perfect working order, autopsy and toxicology revealed no medical cause, and no equipment or environmental factor explains the failure to deploy. The narrative provides no positive evidence of a specific human error pathway (such as channelized attention, spatial disorientation, or decision error) nor positive evidence of a non-human cause. The reason for the failure to pull remains unknown, making the information insufficient to support either 001HE or 002OC.

The narrative is a tribute to the jumper and contains no factual details about the circumstances of the fatal flight. The cause of death is listed as impact in terminal freefall during proximity flying, but no information is provided about what went wrong—whether it was a flight error, equipment issue, environmental factor, or other cause. There is insufficient information to deduce a human error pathway or to identify a non-human cause.

The jumper was found dead at the base of the wall with his canopy deployed, but nobody witnessed the jump, no gear inspection was conducted before authorities removed the equipment, and weather conditions were not reported. All proposed explanations (off-heading, packing issue, thermals) are explicitly described as speculation with no supporting evidence. There is insufficient information to deduce a specific human error pathway or to identify a non-human cause.

The jumper went missing and was found months later at the base of a cliff with no witnesses, no known jump date, no weather information, and no evidence of what occurred during the jump. While the site is known for unpredictable crosswinds, there is no positive evidence establishing whether the fatal outcome resulted from human error or from environmental factors beyond the jumper's control. The narrative explicitly states it is impossible to determine what happened.

The narrative provides virtually no information about the jumper, their equipment, experience, the jump itself, or what went wrong. The only known facts are that a parachute was seen descending near Trollveggen and a body was recovered. There is insufficient information to deduce a human error pathway or to identify a non-human cause.

The jumper deployed his pilot chute too late, resulting in impact at line stretch. Regardless of the debated underlying cause (sun glare, suit performance misjudgment, or attention failure), the late deployment constitutes a human error in altitude awareness or deployment timing. The narrative confirms the pull was very late, which is a performance/skill-based or decision error by the jumper.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unclear cause of late deployment
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper deployed too late and impacted at line stretch. Multiple competing explanations exist (sun glare causing altitude misjudgment, steeper-than-expected descent rate from tracking suit, or channelized attention on landing area), but there is no video and the narrative explicitly states there is no way to determine whether this was a visual issue, a suit performance issue, or an attention issue. The active error (late pull) is established but its mechanism cannot be classified.

L0_description: The cause of death is listed as malfunction due to misrigging, and the narrative explicitly states it was not a manufacturer error, indicating the malfunction resulted from the jumper's own rigging or packing actions. This constitutes a human error in equipment preparation that led to the fatal malfunction. Additionally, the jumper spent 8 seconds fighting the malfunction without resolving it, suggesting a possible performance error in malfunction response, though the specific canopy state could not be confirmed from the video.

L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper experienced a malfunction after a clean deployment bag extraction at 3 seconds. GoPro footage shows 8 seconds of violent kicking and riser fighting before impact, but the chest-mounted camera never captures the canopy, so the type of malfunction cannot be determined. The cause of death is listed as malfunction/misrigging and it is stated this was not a manufacturer error, indicating a human rigging or packing error. However, the narrative does not provide enough detail to distinguish whether the error was a decision error (e.g., choosing an incorrect equipment configuration) or a performance/skill-based error (e.g., incorrect packing or rigging technique, or inadequate malfunction response).
